coredata.dc:title: Embedding dual fluoroprobe in metal-organic frameworks for continuous visual recognition of Pb2+ and PO4 3- via fluorescence 'turn-off-on' response: Agar test paper and fingerprint
coredata.dc:creator: Yi, Kuiyu
Zhang, Lei
coredata.dc:description: A novel dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescence (RF) probe CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 has been successfully constructed by employing a simple and effective strategy for in situ encapsulating carbon dots (CDs) and thioglycolic acid-modified CdTe quantum dots (QDs) into porous metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) "cage". The dual responsive colorimetric fluorescence probe was developed for the ultra-high selectivity and sensitivity continuous detection of Pb2+ (turn OFF) and PO4 3− (turn ON) in biological samples. Blue CDs acts as a stable internal standard emission, the emission color of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 changes from red to blue with introducing Pb2+, fluorescence of probe is quenched because of the binding of Pb2+ ions to thioglycolic acid on the surface of probe and e- transfer from the photoexcited QDs to Pb2+ ions, color can be recovered after the adding PO4 3− to CDs/QDs@ZIF-8-Pb2+ system, which could take away Pb2+ ions from the surface of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8. More importantly, fabricated agar test papers was also successfully applied in visual detection of Pb2+ and PO4 3 - in real samples, which can implement without instrument-specific calibration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent
Metal-organic frameworks
Visual detection
Test paper
Fingerprint

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst misctext primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Pb2+/PO4 3−ions by using a turn off / turn on approach
Detection of targets in an aqueous medium
Detection of targets on agar test papers
Application of probe in sweat fingerprint development

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) CDs, (B) QDs, (C) ZIF-8, and (D) CDs/QDs@ZIF-8.

(A) X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of (a) ZIF-8 and (b) CDs/QDs@ZIF-8; (B) Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8; (C) Fourier infrared spectroscopy of (a)ZIF-8, (b) CDs/QDs@ZIF-8, (c) CDs, and (d) QDs; (D) Fluorescence intensity of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 (black line), CDs/QDs (red line), CDs (blue line), QDs (pink line), supernatant (orange line), and ZIF-8 (green line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) and (B) The fluorescence quenching response of Pb2+ to CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 and CDs/QDs, respectively, inset of (A): the color change process under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp; (C) The linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity (F612/F441)0/(F612/F441) and the concentration of Pb2+; (D) and (E) The fluorescence recover response of PO4 3− to CDs/QDs@ZIF-8-Pb2+ and CDs/QDs-Pb2+, respectively, inset of (D): the color change process under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp; (F) The linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity (F612/F441)/(F612/F441)0 and the concentration of PO4 3−.

(A) The relative fluorescence intensity of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 in the presence of a variety of substances (red bars), and the subsequent addition of Pb2+ (blue bars); (B) The relative fluorescence intensity of Pb2+ quenched CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 in the presence of some coexisted substances (red bars), and the subsequent addition of PO4 3− (blue bars). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

The visual detection of Pb2+ and PO4 3− in human serum using ratiometric fluorescent probe-based agar test papers. The color change of test paper after the addition of Pb2+ (A), and subsequent addition PO4 3− (B) were taken under a 365 nm UV lamp.

Photographs of fingerprint detection with CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 as fluorescent labeling markers on the surface of glass before (a), after the addition of 60 μmol/L Pb2+ (b), and subsequent the addition of 50 μmol/L PO4 3− under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp.
Schematic diagram of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 ratiometric probe and visual identification principle of Pb2+ and PO4 3−.
Determination of Pb2+ in serum, and urine samples (n = 5).
Determination of PO4 3− in serum, and urine samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Embedding dual fluoroprobe in metal-organic frameworks for continuous visual recognition of Pb2+ and PO4 3- via fluorescence 'turn-off-on' response: Agar test paper and fingerprint

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, 66 Chongshan Middle Road, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110036, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, 66 Chongshan Middle Road, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110036, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescence (RF) probe CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 has been successfully constructed by employing a simple and effective strategy for in situ encapsulating carbon dots (CDs) and thioglycolic acid-modified CdTe quantum dots (QDs) into porous metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) "cage". The dual responsive colorimetric fluorescence probe was developed for the ultra-high selectivity and sensitivity continuous detection of Pb2+ (turn OFF) and PO4 3− (turn ON) in biological samples. Blue CDs acts as a stable internal standard emission, the emission color of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 changes from red to blue with introducing Pb2+, fluorescence of probe is quenched because of the binding of Pb2+ ions to thioglycolic acid on the surface of probe and e- transfer from the photoexcited QDs to Pb2+ ions, color can be recovered after the adding PO4 3− to CDs/QDs@ZIF-8-Pb2+ system, which could take away Pb2+ ions from the surface of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8. More importantly, fabricated agar test papers was also successfully applied in visual detection of Pb2+ and PO4 3 - in real samples, which can implement without instrument-specific calibration.

ce:section.ce:para:
As a kind of metal ion with high bioaccumulation, refractory and severe toxicity, Pb2+ represents a serious threat to the health of human beings, animals, and plants (Liu et al., 2014; Needleman, 2004). Pb2+ enters the body mainly through drinking water and diet, which can seriously damage the blood system and brain, kidney and nervous system functions. Phosphate ion (PO4 3
− ), as an important metabolic and genetic material in life system, is associated with many important physiological and pathological processes (Dwivedi et al., 2011; Wygladacz et al., 2008). PO4 3
− levels in biological fluids can be used to diagnose certain diseases, such as higher PO4 3
− concentrations that are directly related to cardiovascular disease and acute renal failure. Although a variety of analytical strategies for trace Pb2+ and PO4 3- detection have been reported (Bora et al., 2015; Cheng et al., 2009; Colina and Gardiner, 1999; Gilbert et al., 2011; Guzinski et al., 2013; Islam et al., 2014), it is still imperative to develop a simple, efficient, and real-time detection technology to measure Pb2+ and PO4 3
− found in the organism to provide enhanced in clinical diagnostic.

Fluorescent sensors with high simplicity and sensitivity have been considered as a powerful tool in clinical diagnosis and biological label (Gao et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2016). A general method is to develop the fluorescent probes that can produce the fluorescent "turn-off" and "turn-on" responses to Pb2+ or PO4 3
− , such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) (Guo et al., 2017; Ji et al., 2018, 2017; Yang et al., 2015.), semiconductor quantum dots (Liu et al., 2014; Mohamed Ali et al., 2007.), graphene quantum dots (Bai et al., 2013; Bian et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2013a), gold/sliver nanoclusters (Bain et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2015a), organic dyes (Dwivedi et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2013b; Sun et al., 2017; Wan et al., 2017.), and Zinc Complex (Ambrosi et al., 2009; Khatua et al., 2009.). However, the above detection of Pb2+ or PO4 3
− ions is basically based on a single fluorescent chemo sensor. Up to date, there are still few reports on the detection of Pb2+or PO4 3- ions based on ratiometric sensing (Sinha et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2017), which are preferred to eliminate interference from environmental effects and improve detection accuracy (Domaille et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2011). Further, the subsequent detection of Pb2+and PO4 3- through ratiometric fluorescence (RF) has also not been reported. If the continuous detection of Pb2+and PO4 3
− ions can be realized under the same test conditions, the method is simple to operate, and the workload of probe synthesis can be reduced, which will be beneficial to energy saving and emission reduction. There is an urgent need to develop a new method for continuous detection based on RF probes. Moreover, the development of fluorescent test papers is promising for the simple and inexpensive assays of target ions. At present, most of reported fluorescent test papers only can provide the yes/no detection by fluorescent brightness (Li et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2015b). The accurate quantification by dosage-dependent color responses on fluorescent test papers still need to be further developed.

In this paper, an encapsulation strategy that allows two types of carbon dots (CDs) and modified quantum dots (QDs) to be incorporated within crystals of ZIF-8 have been developed for recognition of Pb2+ and subsequent PO4 3
− , and as-prepared test paper for colorimetry-based quantification of Pb2+ and PO4 3- in biological medium.

Sodium borohydride (96 %), tellurium powder (99.99 %), sodium phosphate, urea, citric acid and thioglycolic acid were purchased from Sinopharm Shenyang Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Cadmium chloride, sodium hydroxide was purchased from Shenyang Laibo Electronics Co., Ltd. Lead ions (1000 μg/mL) were obtained from the China National Standards Center. All solutions in the experiment were prepared in ultrapure water and all chemicals used were of analytical grade.

Fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Cary Eclipse 300 fluorescence spectrometer (Varian, USA). The ultraviolet absorption spectrum was recorded on a TU-1900 ultraviolet absorption spectrometer (Puxi Analytic Instrument Ltd of Beijing, China). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy was recorded with a spectrometer (Nicolet 5700, Thermo Fisher). Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan) was used to characterize the surface topography of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 composites. The crystal structure and phase purity of 3D CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 were identified by X-ray diffractometry (XRD, Bruker D8, Germany) with CuKa-radiation.

According to the method reported in the literature, some modifications were made to synthesize thioglycolic acid-modified CdTe quantum dots (Li et al., 2007). Specifically, 40.0 mL of 0.02 mol/L CdCl2 solution, and 0.14 mL of thioglycolic acid was added to a 100 mL three-necked flask, and then the pH was adjusted to 11.5 with a 2.0 mol/L NaOH solution. After introducing nitrogen for 20 min, a volume of NaHTe was quickly injected into the solution under continuous stirring. The molar ratio of Cd2+: TGA: HTe– is 1:2:0.25. The generated CdTe original solution was charged into a Teflon reactor, and heated in a constant temperature drying oven at 140 °C for a certain time to obtain CdTe fluorescent quantum dots. The synthesized quantum dots were purified by centrifugation, and the obtained CdTe quantum dots were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min with three volumes of methanol, and repeated three times to remove unreacted precursors such as Cd2+ and sulfhydryl compounds. The precipitation was dried by vacuum dryer. The dried quantum dots were dissolved in 40 mL distilled water and stored in 4 °C refrigerator for later use.

Carbon dots were prepared according to the reference method (Tian et al., 2017). Briefly, citric acid (1 g) and urea (2 g) were accurately weighed and dissolved in 10 mL of water. After complete dissolution, it was transferred to a Teflon reactor, heated in an oven at 160 °C for 4 h, and then cooled to room temperature. The resulting solution was further mixed with 20 mL of ethanol, centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 1 min, and then precipitated for 10 min. The residual small molecules were washed off twice, and then dried at 60 °C to obtain carbon dots. The resulting carbon dots were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water for further use.

CDs/CdTe QDs@ ZIF-8 was synthesized in situ by the classical synthesis method of ZIF-8 in references (Venna et al., 2010). Specifically, 2-methylimidazole (HmIm, 8.0 mmol) was dissolved in 15 mL anhydrous methanol. After 2-methylimidazole was completely dissolved, 2.5 mL of CdTe quantum dot solution, and 40 μL of CDs solution were added. In another beaker, zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn (NO3)2·6H2O, 1.0 mmol) was dissolved in 15 mL anhydrous methanol. After dissolved, the methanol solution of Zn (NO3)2 was poured into the methanol solution of HmIm at one time, and the mixture was stirred rapidly for 6 h at room temperature. The obtained suspension was centrifugally separated and washed with anhydrous methanol for three times to remove unreacted residues. Finally, CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 was placed in a vacuum drying oven and dried at 60 °C for later use.

22.5 mg CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 was accurately weighed and dispersed in 10 mL 0.02 mol/L pH = 7.4 HEPES buffer solution as working solution.2.4 mL of 0.02 M pH 7.4 HEPES buffer solution, 400 μL CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 working solutions were added in 4 mL test tube with plug, and then 200 μL different concentrations of Pb2+ were added. After vortexed the mixture for 20 s, the mixture was incubated at room temperature for 15 min, and the excitation wavelength was determined at 365 nm.

2.2 mL 0.02 M pH 7.4 HEPES buffer solution, 400 μL CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 working solution, and 200 μL 1.5 mmol/L Pb2+ were added in 4 mL plugged test tube in turn. The mixture was whirled for 20 s, flowed by incubated at room temperature for 15 min, and then incubated with different concentration of PO4 3
− solution at 200 μL. The mixture was re-swirled for 20 s, incubated for the other 12 min, and the excitation wavelength was set to 365 nm.

Specifically, 0.4 g of agar powder was first dissolved in 10 mL of boiling distilled water. Transfer 1.5 mL of the agar solution to a small 25 mL beaker. Then, place the small beaker in a 60 °C constant temperature water bath and mix well with 2 mL CDs/QDs@ZIF-8. Then, the plate was frozen at 18 °C for 3 min, and the resulting gel was cut into a spherical shape. Then, the agar slices were immersed in a series of 2.5 mL solutions containing different concentrations of Pb2+ and PO4 3
− , soaked for 15 min, taken out for photography under 365 nm UV radiation.

First, wash your hands with soap three times, dry them in natural condition, and wear disposable PE gloves. After 10 min, remove the gloves and press the fingerprint on a glass. CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 solution was sprayed onto the fingerprint ridge surface by 50 mL spray bottle, so that it fully adsorbed onto the fingerprint texture. The fluorescent fingerprint was then placed under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp and photographed with a Nikon D80 digital camera. Subsequently, after uniformly spraying a certain concentration of Pb2+ and PO4 3
− onto the fingerprint surface, and the fluorescent fingerprint was photographed under ultraviolet light, respectively.

The morphology and microstructure of QDs, CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 were characterized by TEM, whose results are shown in Fig. 1 . For QDs and CDs, spherical in uniform distribution with particle size of about 4 and 2 nm was observed, respectively (Fig. 1A, and B). As seen in Fig. 1C and D, both ZIF-8 and CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 exhibited regular rhombic dodecahedral structure and an average diameter of approximately 110 nm.

The crystallinity and phase purity of ZIF-8 and CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 was further confirmed by XRD analysis (Fig. 2 A. All the characteristic diffraction peaks of ZIF-8 and CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 match well with the simulated patterns of ZIF-8 CCDC: 823083. The results not only reveal that the synthesized samples have high phase purity with rhombic dodecahedron structure, but also demonstrate that the loading of QDs and CDs does not impact the crystal configuration of ZIF-8. The reason for this phenomenon may be due to the extremely small particle size of QDs and CDs, and most of which are embedded in the pores of ZIF-8.

In order to further prove that the CDs and QDs are loaded in the ZIF-8, the EDS of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 are measured. As shown in Fig. 2B, the as-prepared products contain six elements of Cd, Te, S, O, N and C, confirming that the CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 are successfully synthesized.

Fig. 2C shows the FT-IR spectra of the synthesized ZIF-8, CDs/QDs@ZIF-8, CDs, and QDs. As seen, the FT-IR spectra of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 was similar with that of pure ZIF-8. Moreover, the characteristic peaks of CDs, and QDs were not observed in the spectra of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8. This may be due to the fact that there is no interaction among CDs, QDs, and ZIF-8, and the content of CDs and QDs is very small, which does not change the characteristic groups of ZIF-8.

The CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 were also characterized by fluorescence emission spectrum. Compared with the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, QDs, CDs/QDs and ZIF-8, it was found that the fluorescence emission peaks of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 at 441 and 612 nm were consistent with those of CDs and QDs, respectively (Fig. 2D). However, for pure ZIF-8, no significant fluorescence emission peaks in the same range appeared. The results show that the fluorescence of the CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 are derived from CDs and QDs rather than from ZIF-8, which also indicate that the fluorescence properties of CDs and QDs in ZIF-8 are still retained.

In addition, the CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 was thoroughly washed with deionized water, and the supernatant after centrifugation was subjected to fluorescence detection under the previous test conditions, and the fluorescence intensity was almost straight (Fig. 2D orange line), this fully proved that all QDs and CDs were encapsulated into the pores of ZIF-8 and not left in solution, indicating successful synthesis of the CDs/QDs@ZIF-8. Interestingly, at the emission peak, the wavelength difference between the two emission peaks of the CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 (441 and 612 nm) reached 171 nm. This can effectively avoid interference between the dual-emission peaks during fluorescence detection of the analytes, which is more advantageous for actual detection using dual-emission RF probes.

In order to obtain the optimal experimental results, the factors affecting the detection of Pb2+ and PO4 3
− , including pH (Figure S1), reaction time (Figure S2 and S3), stability(Figure S4), and Pb2+ concentration (Figure S5), were investigated, which were given in support information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pb2+/PO4 3−ions by using a turn off / turn on approach
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different Pb2+ concentrations on the fluorescence spectra of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 were investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 A. As can be seen in the figure, the fluorescence emission peaks of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 are located at 441 nm and 612 nm, respectively, which are attributed to the maximum fluorescence emission peaks of CDs and QDs, respectively. After the addition of Pb2+, the fluorescence intensity at 612 nm of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 was significantly quenched, while the peak at 441 nm remained unchanged. Meanwhile, the color of the reaction system gradually changed from red to blue with a decrease in fluorescence intensity at 612 nm. For CDs/QDs, the same quenching law as CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 was obtained with the addition of Pb2+, as shown in Fig. 3B.

It was observed that the relationship between (F612/F441)0/(F612/F441) and Pb2+ conformed to the conventional Stern-Volmer relationship (Zhao et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2019). The relationship is described by F0/F = 1 + KSV [Q], where F0 and F is the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent probe before and after adding quencher, KSV is the quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of Pb2+. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the fluorescence quenching degree of CDs/QDs/ZIF-8 and CDs/QDs was linear with the concentration of 0.04―60 μmol/L and 0.2―55 μmol/L with the linear correlation coefficient 0.9987 and 0.9982 for Pb2+, respectively (Fig. 3C). The KSV was found to be 8.46 × 104 and 5.87 × 104 L/mol, respectively, which clearly indicated that CDs/QDs/ZIF-8 acts as a highly sensitive detector for Pb2+ identification. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated according to the following formula: LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the blank CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 or CDs/QDs measured 9 times standard deviation (n = 9), k is the slope of the standard curve. The detection limit was obtained 2.35 nmol/L and 13.07 nmol/L, respectively. Moreover, the relative standard deviation was 3.2 and 4.0 % for parallel measurement of 15 μmol/L Pb2+ 11 times (n = 11) with CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 and CDs/QDs, respectively.

After the addition of PO4 3
− into the CDs/QDs@ZIF-8–Pb2+ and CDs/QDs–Pb2+system, there was a very good enhancement in fluorescence of QDs, respectively (Fig. 3D and E). The effect of different concentration of PO4 3
− on the recovery of fluorescence intensity of the system was shown in Fig. 3F, it clearly showed that the fluorescence intensity of QDs in CDs/QDs@ZIF-8–Pb2+ and CDs/QDs–Pb2+ linearly increases with successive addition of PO4 3
− in the range of 0.25―50 μmol/L and 0.8―50 μmol/L with the correlation coefficient 0.9986 and 0.9984, respectively. Moreover, the detection limit (3σ/k) based on CDs/QDs@ZIF-8–Pb2+ and CDs/QDs–Pb2+ was 9.42 and 56.71 nmol/L (n = 9), respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of 20 μmol/L PO4 3
− (n = 11) was 3.5 and 4.3 % after 11 paralleled measurements via CDs/QDs@ZIF-8–Pb2+ and CDs/QDs–Pb2+, respectively. The results show that CDs/QDs@ZIF probe has better sensitivity and reproducibility. This is mainly due to the porous structure of MOFs, which makes CDs/QDs packaged in "cages" play a full role in avoiding agglomeration, as well as, the porous MOFs structures could also provide a large specific surface to increase the contact area between the probe and targets, which improved the sensitivity of the sensor (Hao et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2018).

In order to better understand the detection mechanism of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 for Pb2+ and PO4 3−, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs/QDs@ZIF-8, CDs/QDs@ZIF-8+Pb2+, and CDs/QDs@ZIF-8+Pb2++PO4 3− were compared. In this system, the fitting of fluorescence lifetime is in accordance with the typical double exponential model: F(t)= b1exp(−t/τ1)+ b2exp(−t/τ2), where τ1 and τ2 represent the time constant, and b1 and b2 represent the amplitude of the component, respectively. The calculation formula of average lifetime is τ= (b1τ1 2 + b2τ2 2)/ (b1τ1 + b2τ2) (Zeng et al., 2008). As shown in Figure S6, the average fluorescence lifetime of QDs in CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 is 84.57 ns. When 15 μmol/L Pb2 + is added, the fluorescence lifetime decreases to 39.55 ns. When 10 μmol/L PO4 3− is added, the fluorescence lifetime is recovered to 64.71 ns. At the same time, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs in CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 was 5.69 ns, and there was almost no change after adding Pb2+ and PO4 3− (Figure S7). This may be due to that Pb2+ combined with the sulfhydryl group on the surface of the QDs increases the non-radiative transition (Ding et al., 2014; Osovsky et al., 2007), and decreases the fluorescence lifetime of the QDs. After adding PO4 3 -, the sulfhydryl group-Pb2+ on the surface of QDs was destroyed, the non-radiative transition was reduced, and the fluorescence lifetime was recovered.

The reason for turn off/on maybe due to electron transfer between QDs and Pb2+, as well as PO4 3
− has a strong affinity for Pb2+, specifically, thioglycolic acid on the surface of QDs can bind Pb2+ and capture electrons directly from the conduction band of photoexcited QDs, which facilitates the non-radiative recombination between the excited state electrons in the conduction band of QDs and the holes in the valence band, thus leading to the fluorescence quenching of charge transfer in QDs (Chen and Rosenzweig, 2002; Qu et al., 2014; Vázquez-González and Carrillo-Carrion, 2014), as shown in Scheme 1 . When PO4 3
− was introduced into to the QDs–Pb2+ system, PO4 3
− can strongly combine with Pb2+(Pb3(PO4)2, Ksp< 8.0 × 10–43), which makes Pb2+ on the surface of CdTe QDs was desorbed, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of the CdTe quantum dots. It can be concluded that the CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 RF probe improves the detection sensitivity of Pb2+ and PO4 3
− , and proves that the structure of ZIF-8 has a good ability to enrich the analytes.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of RF probes to Pb2+, 23 different substances were used as interferences under the same conditions to investigate their response to CDs@QDs@ZIF-8, as shown in Fig. 4 A. By adding 25 μM Pb2+, F612/F441 quenched 68.7 %. However, other substances such as 1 mM of K+, Na+, Ca2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, glucose, maltose, fructose, lactose, ascorbic acid, urea, uric acid, creatinine, and 30 μmol/L of Ba2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, l-cysteine, glutathione, have much lesser influence on F612/F441 than Pb2+. Then, adding 25 μmol/L Pb2+ to each of the above systems, it can be observed that the interference ions have little effect on the detection of Pb2+, indicating that the RF probe has a good selectivity for Pb2+.

Similarly, the “turn-on” probe was used to evaluate the selectivity of PO4 3
− recognition. The effects of 20 different interfering substances on the fluorescence of composite probes were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4B, the F612/F441 of QDs/CDs@MOFs-Pb2+ sensors were recorded by adding different substances (1 mmol/L of SCN–, CH3COO–, HCO3–,NO3–, Cl–, glucose, maltose, fructose, lactose, ascorbic acid, urea, uric acid, creatinine, and 60 μmol/L of F–, SO3 2–, CO3 2–, I–, SO4 2–, Br–, C2O4 2–, S2–, and 30 μmol/L of L-cysteine, glutathione), respectively. The F612/F441 was recovered by adding PO4 3
− , while the other 20 interfering substances had little influence on the fluorescence intensity ratio F612/F441. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity was recovered to almost the same level via adding 40 μmol/L PO4 3
− to the above systems. In view of this phenomenon, we can infer that the interfering substances will not have potential interference in the detection of PO4 3
− . The results show that the method has good selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of targets in an aqueous medium
ce:section.ce:para:
The established method has been applied to the determination of Pb2+ and PO4 3
− in serum, and urine, respectively, and the real sample analysis procedures were given in supporting information. As shown in Table 1 and 2
, the recoveries of each group were between 94.0–106.5 % at three different concentrations, and the RSD was close to 5 %, indicating the accuracy and precision of the method for determining Pb2+ and PO4 3
− concentration in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of targets on agar test papers
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to improve the practicability of the detection method, we attempts to embed CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 into a solid matrix to make test papers that are easy to carry and use. The blank test paper is non-fluorescent, which can avoid the influence of visual recognition. As shown in Fig. 5 A(a), under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, the regular changes of red, pink, purple to blue were observed when agar test paper was exposed to standard solution of Pb2+with different concentrations (0−60 μmol/L). For the test paper used to detect PO4 3
− (0−50 μmol/L), the opposite color change was displayed, as seen in Fig. 5B(a). With the typical dosages of Pb2+ and PO4 3
− , the consecutive color variations can be easily distinguished by naked eyes.

In addition, the visual detection of Pb2+ and PO4 3
− in human serum was also investigated. The content and recovery of Pb2+ and PO4 3
− in serum sample have been determined by RF probe with satisfactory results (Fig. 5A (b), and B (b)). Subsequently, the same samples were tested with fluorescent agar test paper and observed under the ultraviolet lamp. As shown in Fig. 5A (c) and B (c), with the addition of Pb2+ and PO4 3
− content in serum, the color changes of test paper were consistent with those caused by standard Pb2+ and PO4 3
− , respectively. The visual detection technology has a very sensitive ability to distinguish dosages with naked eyes, which provides a new idea for clinical diagnosis and biological system detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of probe in sweat fingerprint development
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to evaluate the application prospects of the designed probe for fluorescence labeling in biological systems, the probe was directly used for fluorescence detection of Pb2+ and PO4 3
− in sweat latent fingerprint. It can be observed from Fig. 6 a that the fingerprints incubated with CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 immediately show a bright red fluorescence with smooth lines and detailed features, which is sufficient to mask the blue fluorescence of the CDs. When 60 μmol/L Pb2+ is added, the fluorescence of the QDs as a signal tag in the dual-emitting fluorescent probe is quenched, and the blue fluorescence of the CDs is slowly highlighted (Fig. 6b), which is easily captured by the naked eyes. Interestingly, the blue fluorescent fingerprint observed after the addition of 50 μmol/L PO4 3
− returned to red (Fig. 6c). The connection between the CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 probe and the fingerprint is achieved by covalent coupling of the functional group on the surface of the probe with the residual amino acid in the fingerprint, which makes it possible to apply the probe to the field of biological detection.

In this work, as-prepared CDs/QDs@ZIF8 probe for the selective detection of Pb2+ and subsequent PO4 3
− discrimination in biological medium has been developed. The CDs/QDs@ZIF-8 upon interaction with Pb2+ ions selectively, exhibited quenched emission, fluorescence “turn–off” response. Upon successive addition of PO4 3
− , showed enhanced emission, fluorescence “turn–on” response. The consecutive bright fluorescent color variations from red to blue and blue to red appeared in the medium was sensitive to the naked-eyes, and the possible mechanism “On–Off–On” approach was also explained in detail. Generated agar test paper has a satisfying performance for the Pb2+ and PO4 3- monitoring in real samples, which can give a clear judgment whether targets is at a normal or abnormal level in clinical diagnosis. Also the probe has been utilized to detect Pb2+ and PO4 3
− in sweat latent fluorescent fingerprint that can be developed under UV light.

Kuiyu Yi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Lei Zhang: Resources, Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 51672116), Liaoning Province Science and Technology Planning Project (No. 20180551203), the Liaoning Provincial Department of Education Innovation Team Project for Innovative Talent Training (No. LT2017010). The authors also thank their colleagues and other students who participated in this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2020.122141.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
